[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.maker has previously canceled the root via cancelRoot, trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] and observe that HashLib.isLeaf (O(proof.length) keccak256 operations) executes fully before the cheap isRootCanceled storage read reverts, causing unnecessary gas consumption proportional to proof.length for every take attempt against a canceled root? Proof idea: fuzz proof.length in [1..20] with a pre-canceled root and measure gas; assert that swapping the check order (isRootCanceled before isLeaf) reduces gas for the canceled-root path.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that ratifierData encodes a proof array of length 21, trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash(21)] and observe that the revert selector is HashLib.TreeTooHigh rather than IEcrecoverRatifier.InvalidProof, causing a behavior divergence where callers cannot distinguish an oversized proof from a structurally invalid proof? Proof idea: unit test with proof.length == 21 and assert revert selector == HashLib.TreeTooHigh.selector, then compare with proof.length == 20 and wrong root which reverts with InvalidProof.selector.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that ratifierData is ABI-malformed (e.g., empty bytes or truncated encoding), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> abi.decode(ratifierData, (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[]))] and observe a generic low-level revert with no custom error selector, causing a behavior divergence where off-chain tooling cannot distinguish malformed input from InvalidProof or InvalidSignature? Proof idea: unit test with ratifierData = hex'' and assert revert has no selector matching any IEcrecoverRatifier error.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that a valid (v, r, s) signature exists for a digest, construct a second valid signature (v', r', s') for the same digest by negating s modulo the secp256k1 curve order and flipping v, trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> ecrecover(digest, v', r', s')] and observe that both signatures pass verification, causing a behavior divergence where the contract accepts high-s malleable signatures contrary to EIP-2 best practice? Proof idea: fuzz (v, r, s) from vm.sign, compute (v^1, r, n-s), call isRatified with both and assert both return CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged caller (msg.sender == maker), under the precondition that isRootCanceled[maker][root] is already true, call cancelRoot(maker, root) again and observe that the function succeeds, emits a duplicate CancelRoot event, and performs a warm SSTORE of true->true, causing unnecessary gas cost and duplicate event emission that may confuse off-chain indexers tracking cancellation state? Proof idea: call cancelRoot twice with the same (maker, root), assert two CancelRoot events are emitted and measure gas of second call.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.market.collateralParams has 128 entries (the protocol maximum), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.hashOffer -> HashLib.hashMarket] and observe that the gas cost of isRatified scales linearly with collateralParams.length due to the inner loop in hashMarket, causing a non-critical but measurable gas overhead for markets with many collateral assets? Proof idea: fuzz collateralParams.length in [1, 16, 64, 128] and measure isRatified gas; assert gas grows linearly.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.callbackData is a large byte array (e.g., 10 KB), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.hashOffer -> keccak256(offer.callbackData)] and observe that the gas cost of isRatified scales with callbackData.length, causing unnecessary gas overhead for offers with large callback payloads? Proof idea: fuzz callbackData.length in [0, 100, 1000, 10000] and measure isRatified gas; assert gas grows with callbackData.length.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that _signer != offer.maker (i.e., a delegated signer is used), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> ecrecover -> IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer)] and observe that isRatified makes two external calls to MIDNIGHT (one STATICCALL for isAuthorized) compared to one when _signer == offer.maker, causing variable gas cost depending on whether the maker or a delegate signs? Proof idea: measure gas of isRatified with maker-signed vs delegate-signed ratifierData and assert the delegate path costs more gas.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that the domainSeparator is recomputed via keccak256(abi.encode(EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH, block.chainid, address(this))) on every isRatified call rather than being cached as an immutable, trigger repeated calls to isRatified and observe that each call pays for a fresh keccak256 and abi.encode, causing unnecessary gas cost that could be eliminated by caching the domainSeparator as an immutable (since block.chainid is already captured at deploy time for non-fork scenarios)? Proof idea: deploy a patched version with immutable domainSeparator and compare gas via differential test.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that proof.length == 0 and root == HashLib.hashOffer(offer), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.isLeaf(root, leafHash, 0, [])] and observe that a single-offer tree (height 0) is accepted with leafIndex == 0, and that the structHash uses offerTreeTypeHash(0) which encodes 'OfferTree(Offer offerTree)' rather than 'OfferTree(Offer[2] offerTree)', causing a behavior divergence in the signed type string for single-offer vs multi-offer trees? Proof idea: sign a root for proof.length==0 and proof.length==1 with the same offer and assert the digests differ.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged caller (msg.sender == maker), under the precondition that root is a bytes32 value that has never been used as a Merkle root for any offer, call cancelRoot(maker, root) and observe that the function succeeds and sets isRootCanceled[maker][root] = true for a root that corresponds to no existing offer, causing a behavior divergence where pre-emptive cancellation of arbitrary roots is allowed without any validation that the root corresponds to a real offer tree? Proof idea: call cancelRoot with root = keccak256('arbitrary') and assert isRootCanceled returns true.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged caller, under the precondition that maker == address(0) and msg.sender == address(0) (impossible in practice but testable via vm.prank), trigger cancelRoot(address(0), root) and observe that isRootCanceled[address(0)][root] is set to true, causing a behavior divergence where the zero address can have roots canceled, which may interact unexpectedly with the Certora invariant addressZeroCantAuthorize? Proof idea: unit test with vm.prank(address(0)) calling cancelRoot(address(0), root) and assert isRootCanceled[address(0)][root] == true.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that leafIndex has bits set at positions >= proof.length (e.g., leafIndex == 2 with proof.length == 1), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.isLeaf -> require(leafIndex >> proof.length == 0, LeafIndexOutOfRange())] and observe that the revert selector is HashLib.LeafIndexOutOfRange rather than IEcrecoverRatifier.InvalidProof, causing a behavior divergence where callers cannot distinguish an out-of-range leaf index from a structurally invalid proof? Proof idea: unit test with leafIndex == 2 and proof.length == 1 and assert revert selector == HashLib.LeafIndexOutOfRange.selector.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that a valid offer is in a Merkle tree of height H signed by the maker, construct a different ratifierData with the same offer but proof.length == H+1 (by appending a dummy sibling and recomputing the root), trigger isRatified with the new ratifierData, and observe that the structHash uses offerTreeTypeHash(H+1) instead of offerTreeTypeHash(H), causing a behavior divergence where the same offer can be ratified under different EIP-712 type strings depending on the proof length provided? Proof idea: build two valid ratifierData for the same offer with proof.length 1 and 2, sign both roots, and assert both pass isRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that sig.v is not in {27, 28} (e.g., sig.v == 0 or sig.v == 1), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> ecrecover(digest, 0, r, s)] and observe that ecrecover returns address(0), causing the revert to use InvalidSignature() rather than Unauthorized(), creating a behavior divergence in error reporting between an invalid v value and an unauthorized signer? Proof idea: fuzz sig.v over [0, 1, 26, 27, 28, 29, 255] and assert revert selector is InvalidSignature for v not in {27,28} and Unauthorized for valid v with wrong signer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that sig.r == bytes32(0) and sig.s == bytes32(0) and sig.v == 27, trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> ecrecover(digest, 27, 0, 0)] and observe that ecrecover returns address(0), causing the revert to use InvalidSignature() rather than Unauthorized(), and confirm that the zero-signature edge case is handled consistently across all v values? Proof idea: unit test with (v=27, r=0, s=0) and assert revert selector == InvalidSignature.selector.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.maker is a valid non-zero address and _signer == offer.maker (maker signs directly), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] and observe that the isAuthorized external call to MIDNIGHT is skipped (short-circuit at _signer == offer.maker), while for a delegated signer the isAuthorized call is always made, causing a gas cost asymmetry between maker-signed and delegate-signed offers that may affect gas estimation in off-chain tooling? Proof idea: measure gas for maker-signed vs delegate-signed isRatified calls and assert the difference equals approximately one STATICCALL to isAuthorized.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that the same offer appears as a leaf in two different Merkle trees (root_A and root_B) both signed by the maker, trigger isRatified with root_A and then with root_B for the same offer, and observe that both succeed independently, causing a behavior divergence where canceling root_A does not invalidate the offer under root_B, requiring the maker to cancel each root separately to fully revoke the offer? Proof idea: build two trees containing the same offer leaf, sign both roots, call isRatified with each, cancel root_A, assert root_B still passes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged operator (authorized via IMidnight.isAuthorized), under the precondition that the maker subsequently revokes the operator's authorization via setIsAuthorized(operator, false, maker), attempt to call cancelRoot(maker, root) and observe that the call reverts with Unauthorized() because the isAuthorized check is live at call time, causing a behavior divergence where an operator who signed a cancellation intent off-chain loses the ability to execute it after deauthorization? Proof idea: authorize operator, deauthorize operator, call cancelRoot as operator and assert Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that the maker authorized a delegate signer and the delegate signed a root, and the maker subsequently revokes the delegate's authorization via setIsAuthorized(delegate, false, maker), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer)] and observe that the take reverts with Unauthorized() even though the signature was valid at signing time, causing a behavior divergence where offer validity is authorization-state-dependent at execution time? Proof idea: sign offer with delegate, revoke delegate, attempt take and assert Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that proof.length == 20 (maximum allowed), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.isLeaf (20 iterations) -> HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash(20)] and observe that isRatified consumes significantly more gas than for proof.length == 0, and that the gas cost scales linearly with proof.length due to the loop in isLeaf, causing a non-critical but measurable gas overhead for deep Merkle trees? Proof idea: fuzz proof.length in [0..20] and measure isRatified gas; assert linear scaling.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that ratifierData encodes a proof array with all elements equal to bytes32(0), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.isLeaf] and observe that the Merkle proof verification proceeds without error (zero-valued siblings are valid bytes32 values), causing a behavior divergence where a proof with all-zero siblings is structurally valid and may match a root if the offer hash and zero siblings produce the expected root? Proof idea: construct a tree with zero-valued siblings, compute the expected root, sign it, and assert isRatified succeeds.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that the EIP-712 domain separator omits the 'name' and 'version' fields (using only chainId and verifyingContract), trigger isRatified and observe that the domain separator diverges from the full EIP-712 standard (which recommends name and version), causing a behavior divergence where wallets that display EIP-712 signing prompts may show incomplete domain information to the maker? Proof idea: compare the domain separator hash against a reference implementation with name/version fields and assert they differ.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that the same (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) ratifierData is submitted for two different takes of the same offer (partial fills), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] twice and observe that isRatified succeeds both times (since it is stateless and does not track consumed signatures), causing a behavior divergence where the ratifier itself does not enforce single-use of a signature (relying entirely on Midnight's consumed tracking)? Proof idea: take the same offer twice with identical ratifierData and assert both isRatified calls return CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Low'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.ratifier != address(this) (the offer's ratifier field points to a different contract), trigger the call sequence [Midnight.take -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified -> HashLib.hashOffer] and observe that the offer hash includes offer.ratifier as a field, so the Merkle proof will fail (InvalidProof) rather than producing a custom error about ratifier mismatch, causing a behavior divergence where the error message does not indicate the root cause? Proof idea: set offer.ratifier to a different address, build a proof for the modified offer, and assert isRatified reverts with InvalidProof.
